topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the locker room seemed to hum with a mocking indifference. I traced the outline of the jersey I hadn't been issued, the fabric cool against my fingertips. Every glance toward the team roster felt like a physical jolt, a sudden spike in my chest that made breathing shallow. He was there, standing near the coach, laughing at something innocuous, and the sound of his voice—deep, resonant—sent a tremor through my hands. I watched him interact with the others, cataloging every casual gesture, every slight tilt of his head. When my name wasn't called, a strange, hollow ache settled in my stomach, a dull throb that made my focus on my own practice feel distant and pointless. I kept replaying the moment he’d looked my way during warm-ups, the brief, electric connection that left me breathless.

[story 2]

My knuckles were white as I gripped the worn leather of my practice ball. The air in the gym felt thick, charged with unspoken possibilities. I kept catching glimpses of her across the court—the way her hair caught the light, the focused intensity in her eyes when she drilled. It was a constant, low-level buzz beneath my skin, making my movements jerky and overly deliberate. When the final selection was announced, a silence seemed to descend, amplifying the sound of my own heartbeat. I forced a smile, a brittle thing that didn't reach my eyes, while internally, I felt a profound disappointment. I lingered near the exit, hoping for a shared moment, a glance that acknowledged the pull I felt toward her, but she was already moving, swallowed by the noise of the celebrating team.

[story 3]

I kept replaying the coach's dismissal, the way his eyes had swept over me, then settled on someone else. A strange heat bloomed in my cheeks, a flush that had nothing to do with exertion. I found myself studying the periphery of the room, hoping for a sign, a flicker of recognition from the person I admired most. Every time the team huddled, I focused on the space where she usually stood, feeling a desperate need to be noticed by her, even if it was just in the background. When the official announcement concluded, the disappointment wasn't just about the team; it was a crushing realization that the magnetic pull I felt toward her was entirely unreciprocated, leaving me stranded in the aftermath of my own intense focus.
